ClinicalDocument.title: Continuity of Care Document - Isabella Brown

value.displayName: for persistent vomiting to enable oral rehydration
if amoxicillin failure or recent antibiotic use

act.text: "She woke up yesterday morning with a fever - I took her temperature and it was 101.5. She's been complaining that her head and body ache, and she's just been lying around, won't really eat much. I gave her some ibuprofen this morning and it came down a bit, but it keeps going back up. I'm mostly worried because the fever won't stay down and she's never been like this before."

Fever: Clinical presentation consistent w/ fever: Pt reports elevated temp; vitals show HR 80 (appropriate for age/fever state). Normal HEENT & clear lungs B/L exclude acute URTI/pneumonia as primary etiology, suggesting viral syndrome or early infection requiring further workup (labs, urinalysis) to r/o UTI, bacteremia, or occult source.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"She started throwing up yesterday morning, maybe around 9 or 10 AM, and has had it about 5 or 6 times since then. She also has diarrhea—we've been keeping track because we were worried. We've been giving her small sips of water and some crackers when she felt up to it, but she's not really interested in eating much. My main concern is that she's going to get dehydrated since she's been losing fluids both ways, and I want to make sure this doesn't turn into something more serious."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: mild dehydration evidenced by slightly dry mucous membranes and HR 96 (upper normal range for age), while lungs clear and HEENT/neuro exam unremarkable, excluding alternative diagnoses such as pneumonia or acute abdomen.

- [medication] Ondansetron 6mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill

"She woke up this morning with a really stuffy nose and has been sneezing a lot throughout the day. We noticed her eyes are itchy too—she keeps rubbing them. We gave her some children's allergy medicine from the cabinet about an hour ago, but it hasn't helped much yet. I'm mainly worried because she has soccer practice tomorrow and she can barely breathe through her nose right now."

Allergic Rhinitis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ allergic rhinitis: normal vital signs (HR 98) & clear lungs rule out acute infection/complications; normal HEENT exam supports allergic etiology rather than structural abnormality or sinusitis.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 8 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and sore throat. We gave her some ibuprofen and she felt a little better, but today the fever came back and she's complaining that her head hurts. She's been drinking okay but won't eat much—just had some applesauce this morning. I'm mainly worried because she seems really tired and just wants to lay on the couch, which isn't like her at all."

Viral Syndrome: History and exam consistent w/ viral syndrome: normal vital signs (HR 91) and clear lungs exclude bacterial pneumonia; normocephalic HEENT w/o focal findings rules out meningitis or other serious infection. Benign exam supports conservative management and supportive care.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She started complaining of a tummy ache yesterday afternoon and threw up twice last night. This morning she had diarrhea and felt hot, so I took her temperature and it was over 101. She's been refusing food and barely drinking anything, just sipping water here and there. I gave her some ginger ale and crackers, but she threw those up too. I'm worried she's getting dehydrated because she hasn't had a wet diaper since this morning."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent with viral gastroenteritis: fever, tachycardia, and mild dehydration (dry mucous membranes) support infectious gastroenteritis etiology; clear lungs and normal HEENT exam exclude respiratory/CNS involvement.

- [medication] Ondansetron 5mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 7 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up this morning with a really stuffy nose and has been sneezing a lot all day. We noticed her nose is all runny and she keeps rubbing her eyes—they look kind of puffy. I gave her some children's allergy medicine from the drugstore about an hour ago, but it hasn't helped much yet. I'm mainly worried because she's got a school field trip tomorrow and I want to make sure this clears up."

Allergic Rhinitis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ allergic rhinitis: normal vital signs & clear lungs r/o acute infection; normal HEENT exam supports allergic etiology rather than structural abnormality or acute sinusitis.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 6 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 5 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI (Parent Narrative): Isabella woke up yesterday morning pulling at her right ear and seemed fussy. She's had a fever since then—we checked it this morning and it was 100.9. She hasn't been eating much, especially on that side, and last night she was really uncomfortable even though we gave her ibuprofen and a warm compress. We're worried it might be an ear infection because she's been tugging at that ear constantly and just seems miserable.

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with AOM: fever, tachycardia, and classic otoscopic findings (erythematous, bulging TM with absent light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy) indicate acute middle ear inflammation with effusion.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1850mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1850mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 210mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy ever since. She's complaining that her throat hurts and won't eat much, just wants to drink. We gave her some Tylenol and she felt a little better for a bit, but the fever keeps coming back. We're mostly worried because she's never been this cranky before and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: History and exam consistent w/ viral syndrome: pt presents w/ viral prodrome, vitals stable (HR 91 bpm), and physical exam unremarkable for focal infection—HEENT normocephalic/atraumatic, lungs CTAB bilaterally. Absence of focal findings and benign exam support self-limited viral etiology rather than bacterial infection or other acute pathology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

Parent Narrative: "She woke up yesterday morning with her right eye all red and crusty. There was a lot of gunk in it and she kept saying it was itchy. Today the left eye started getting the same way - both eyes are pretty swollen now and she's been rubbing at them constantly. We've been trying to clean them with a warm washcloth and some saline drops we had at home, but it doesn't seem to be helping much. We're worried it might be something serious or that it'll spread to her brother." --- Key elements included: - Timeline: Started yesterday, progressing to both eyes - Specific observations: Redness, crusting, swelling, itching, discharge - Home treatment attempted: Warm compresses and saline - Parent concern: Severity and transmission risk

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings consistent w/ conjunctivitis: conjunctival injection w/ discharge and eyelid edema on HEENT exam. Normal cornea and VA help exclude keratitis and serious ocular pathology.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has thrown up about four times since then. She's been complaining her stomach hurts and won't eat anything, though she's been drinking a little water. We gave her some Tylenol this morning and she's been resting, but the fever keeps coming back and she just seems really tired and fussy. We're worried she's getting dehydrated since she's not eating or drinking much."

Viral Gastroenteritis: **Clinical Reasoning:**
History and exam consistent with viral gastroenteritis: fever, tachycardia, and slightly dry mucous membranes suggest mild dehydration from GI losses. Absence of respiratory findings and normal HEENT/skin exam exclude alternative diagnoses (URI, otitis, rash-associated illness).

- [medication] Ondansetron 3mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 4 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been coughing on and off since then. She's been pretty fussy and doesn't want to eat much—she'll only drink juice and water. We gave her some children's cold medicine this morning and tried a humidifier in her room last night, but the cough is still there. I'm mainly worried because she's not acting like herself and I want to make sure it's not something more serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: normal vital signs (HR 87) and clear lungs on exam argue against bacterial infection or pneumonia; normocephalic HEENT without focal findings supports self-limited viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty fussy ever since. This morning she had a fever—I checked it and it was 101.5—and she's not really eating or drinking much, just wanting to cuddle. We gave her some children's ibuprofen last night and it brought the fever down a bit, but it keeps coming back. I'm mainly worried because she's so clingy and not herself, and I want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 115) reflects systemic viral illness; normal HEENT and clear lungs B/L rule out localized bacterial infection (otitis media, pneumonia), supporting benign viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She got bit by something yesterday afternoon while playing outside, and by this morning the spot was really swollen and red. We've been putting hydrocortisone cream on it like we do for mosquito bites, but it just keeps getting bigger and itchier. She's been scratching at it constantly and won't leave it alone, which is making it worse. We're worried it's getting infected or that she's having some kind of allergic reaction to whatever bit her."

Insect Bite Reaction: Clinical presentation consistent w/ insect bite rxn: localized skin findings (presumed from CC) w/ normal systemic exam (nl HR, clear lungs, nl HEENT) excludes systemic allergic rxn or anaphylaxis, supporting benign cutaneous diagnosis.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been sneezy all day. This morning she seems more congested and has been pretty fussy, and she won't eat her normal breakfast. We've been giving her lots of fluids and used the humidifier last night, but the congestion doesn't seem to be getting any better. We're mainly worried about whether she has an ear infection or if this will turn into something worse."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lung fields support uncomplicated upper respiratory involvement without evidence of bacterial superinfection or lower respiratory tract disease.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 270mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 180mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She woke up this morning with a really stuffy nose and has been sneezing a lot. She's been rubbing her nose constantly and seems uncomfortable, especially when she's trying to eat or sleep. We gave her some saline drops this morning and that helped a little bit, but the congestion keeps coming back. I'm worried it might turn into an ear infection like last time, so we wanted to get her checked out."

Allergic Rhinitis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ allergic rhinitis: HEENT exam shows typical findings of nasal inflammation/congestion; clear lungs rule out lower respiratory involvement or asthma exacerbation. Vital signs stable (HR 85) w/ no acute distress, supporting uncomplicated allergic etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 3 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"Her left eye started getting red and goopy yesterday morning. At first I thought maybe she poked it or something, but today it got worse—there's yellow stuff crusting over and her eye is really swollen. She keeps rubbing it and saying it hurts. I've been wiping it with a warm washcloth and tried some saline drops, but it's not getting better. I'm worried it's an infection and don't want it to spread to her other eye."

Conjunctivitis: Clinical presentation consistent with conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection with purulent discharge and eyelid edema on exam. Tachycardia and tachypnea likely represent systemic response to infection; clear lungs and normal cornea/VA exclude keratitis and lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia

Isabella started getting these sores on her face about 3 days ago—first just a couple of red bumps near her nose that looked like bug bites. By yesterday they started oozing and crusting over, and now there's more spots popping up on her chin and cheek. I've been cleaning them with soap and water and tried some antibiotic ointment from home, but they just keep spreading. I'm worried it's getting worse and I want to make sure it's nothing serious.

Impetigo: Clinical presentation of localized skin lesions consistent w/ impetigo (honey-crusted pustules/erosions) supported by vital sign abnormalities (HR 112, RR 25) suggesting systemic response to bacterial infection, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude other acute infectious etiologies.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"Her left eye started getting red and crusty yesterday morning. Today it's worse—there's yellow stuff coming out and she keeps rubbing at it. We've been wiping it with a warm washcloth, but it's not getting better. She's acting normal otherwise, playing and eating fine, but I'm worried it might be an infection or something serious."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings support viral conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection with discharge, preauricular lymphadenopathy (classic viral marker), clear cornea, and normal visual acuity. Absence of purulent exudate and systemic symptoms (normal lungs, HR 99) further support viral rather than bacterial etiology.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been really fussy ever since. This morning I noticed she had a fever, and she's been coughing a lot—especially when she's lying down. She's not eating much, just picking at her food, but she's drinking okay. We gave her some acetaminophen and a humidifier in her room last night, but the fever came back this morning, so I brought her in to get checked out."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: fever (100.5°F), tachycardia (HR 100), and tachypnea (RR 27) indicate systemic viral response, while normal HEENT exam and clear bilateral lung fields exclude complications such as otitis media, sinusitis, or lower respiratory tract involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 200mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 130mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 1 year 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Isabella started running a fever yesterday afternoon. Mom noticed she felt warm, took her temperature and it was 102.5°F. She's been fussier than usual and won't eat much, though she's drinking okay. Mom gave her acetaminophen this morning which brought the fever down temporarily, but it came back up. She's concerned because Isabella seems uncomfortable and she wants to make sure it's nothing serious.

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with vital sign abnormalities (HR 113, RR 27) consistent w/ systemic infection; however, normal lung exam and HEENT findings help exclude acute respiratory infection or focal infection as primary etiology. Further workup needed to identify source of fever.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 1 year 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"Isabella woke up fussy yesterday morning and has had a fever since then. She's been pulling at her right ear a lot and won't eat much—just picks at her food. We gave her some ibuprofen and it helped a little bit, but she's still really irritable and won't sleep well. We're worried it might be an ear infection because she just got over a cold last week."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with AOM: fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea suggest systemic infection; otoscopic findings of erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy confirm middle ear inflammation and effusion. Absence of respiratory findings excludes concurrent pneumonia.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1050mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1050mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 120mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

"She started throwing up yesterday afternoon without any warning, and then had diarrhea a few times last night. This morning she had a fever and just seems really tired and clingy—she won't eat anything, just wants to drink water. We've been giving her small sips to keep her from getting dehydrated, but I'm worried because she's usually such a happy baby and now she's just lying around. Her diaper was pretty wet this morning so that's good, but I wanted to make sure nothing serious is going on."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: fever and tachycardia (HR 129) suggest systemic viral infection, while normal lung exam and HEENT findings exclude respiratory etiology or trauma. Absence of focal findings supports viral etiology rather than bacterial infection or surgical abdomen.

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 1 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She had a runny nose and mild cough for a couple days, but this morning she got a fever and started breathing really fast and wheezy. She's been really congested and won't eat much—just keeps coughing, especially at night. We tried saline drops and a humidifier at home, but her breathing sounds worse and she seems tired. We're worried because she's working so hard to breathe and we can see her chest pulling in when she breathes."

Bronchiolitis: History and exam findings are consistent with bronchiolitis: RSV+ rapid antigen confirms viral etiology, while diffuse wheezes/crackles with subcostal/intercostal retractions indicate small airway inflammation and obstruction. Tachypnea (RR 39), tachycardia (HR 134), hypoxia (SpO2 91%), and fever support moderate-to-severe lower respiratory tract infection with increased work of breathing.

- [education] Supportive care with nasal suctioning before feeds - [education] Maintain hydration with frequent small feeds - [education] Monitor for respiratory distress and feeding difficulties - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY if: breathing worsens, color change, stops eating, or appears very ill - [education] Consider hospitalization if SpO2 <90%, severe distress, or poor feeding

"She's had a runny nose for about 3 days, but yesterday she started getting really congested and developed a cough. This morning she woke up with a fever and has been breathing fast and making wheezy sounds when she breathes—it sounds like she's struggling to get air in. We tried saline drops and a humidifier at home, but she's not improving and seems fussier than usual. We're worried because she's not eating well and we can see her chest pulling in when she breathes."

Bronchiolitis: History and exam findings support bronchiolitis diagnosis: Viral prodrome (rhinorrhea) with lower respiratory involvement (diffuse wheezes/crackles), respiratory distress (tachypnea RR 51, nasal flaring, hypoxia SpO2 90%), and systemic signs (fever, tachycardia) are classic for acute bronchiolitis, though RSV rapid antigen negative suggests alternative viral etiology (parainfluenza, rhinovirus, or other respiratory viruses).

- [education] Supportive care with nasal suctioning before feeds - [education] Maintain hydration with frequent small feeds - [education] Monitor for respiratory distress and feeding difficulties - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY if: breathing worsens, color change, stops eating, or appears very ill - [education] Consider hospitalization if SpO2 <90%, severe distress, or poor feeding

HPI - Parent Narrative: "She's been really fussy and running a fever since yesterday afternoon. This morning her temperature was over 102, and she won't eat her usual breakfast—just keeps pulling at her ear and crying. We gave her some infant pain reliever and a cool washcloth, but she's still uncomfortable. I'm worried it might be an ear infection because my older son had the same thing last month."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with acute otitis media (AOM): erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy indicate middle ear inflammation and effusion. Fever and tachycardia/tachypnea are expected systemic inflammatory responses to acute infection in this age group.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 950mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 950mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 100mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 9 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She's been really fussy since yesterday morning and running a fever. We noticed she kept pulling at her right ear and wouldn't eat much at breakfast, so we took her temperature and it was over 100. We gave her some infant acetaminophen and tried a cold teething ring to see if that would help, but she's still uncomfortable and not sleeping well. We're worried it might be an ear infection and want to make sure she's okay."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with AOM: fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea suggest systemic infection; otoscopic findings of erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy are pathognomonic for AOM with MEE. Clear lungs exclude concurrent pneumonia.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 725mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 725mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 80mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started having loose, watery stools yesterday morning—maybe 4 or 5 times since then. She's also been spitting up more than usual and just seems fussy and uncomfortable. We've been trying to keep her hydrated with her regular formula, but she's not taking as much as she normally does. We're mainly worried because she's so little and we want to make sure she doesn't get dehydrated."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent with viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 162) and tachypnea (RR 36) reflect compensatory response to likely mild-moderate dehydration, supported by slightly dry mucous membranes; normal lung exam and HEENT exam exclude alternative diagnoses (pneumonia, meningitis).

- [medication] Ondansetron 1mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She's had a runny nose for about two days now—started with just some clear stuff coming out, and yesterday she got a little more congested. She's been fussier than usual and doesn't seem as interested in her bottles, which worries me. We've been using the bulb syringe to clear her nose and keeping her elevated, but I'm concerned because she sounds so stuffy and I want to make sure it doesn't turn into something worse."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea with mucosal erythema, mild pharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lungs on exam support viral etiology without evidence of lower respiratory involvement or bacterial superinfection. Mild tachycardia (HR 129) and tachypnea (RR 34) are expected compensatory responses in 3-month-old with acute viral URI.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 85mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 2 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 1 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Healthy newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

tr.td: No developmental concerns at this time
No developmental concerns at this time
No developmental concerns at this time
